coredata.dc:title: Preparation of g-C3N4/NS-CQDs composites and their application in the detection of hydrogen peroxide and glucose
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Haiyang
Wang, Zhe
Jin, Lijun
Jin, Lixia
Yan, Fangjie
Li, Ji
Lv, Kuilin
coredata.dc:description: Based on the excellent properties of g-C3N4, co-doped carbon quantum dots (NS-CQDs) were selected to modify it in order to enhance its peroxidase-like activity, and the synthesized NS-CQDs/g-C3N4 nano-enzymes were applied to the detection of common small molecules, such as hydrogen peroxide and glucose, to provide theoretical basis for the construction of g-C3N4 nano-enzymes-based biosensors. FT-IR, SEM, TEM, and other characterization methods determined the composition and morphology of NS-CQDs/g-C3N4. This paper discusses the influence of different morphologies on composites’ catalytic activity. According to the fluorescence spectrum, thecatalytic mechanismfollowsthe hydroxyl radicalmechanism. Under the optimal experimental conditions, the prepared composites were used to detect glucose, and the resultant detection limit of glucose was 1.63 μΜ. The results showed that NS-CQDs/g-C3N4 outperforms most of the nanoenzymes of similar materials, which provides a new idea to broaden its application in nanoenzymes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nitride composites
Nano-enzymes
Hydrogen peroxide
Glucose

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of different forms of g-C3N4
Transmission electron microscopy and energy spectrum analysis
Study on the basic reaction mechanism of g-C3N4/NS-CQDs composites
Cyclic voltammetry was used to determine electron transfer
Detection of hydrogen peroxide and actual samples
Detection of hydrogen peroxide in milk
Detection of glucose and actual samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structure and preparation process of binary composite materials.
XRD patterns of (a) synthetic materials and (b) composites with different NS-CQDs doping ratios.
Infrared spectra of (a) synthetic materials and (b) composites with different NS-CQDs doping ratios.
Raman spectra of composites with different NS-CQDs doping ratios.
The survey XPS spectra of 7NSC-g.
(a) C 1 s spectrum; (b) N 1 s spectrum; (c) S 2p and (d) O 1 s spectrum in the XPS energy spectrum of 7NSC-g.
SEM images of (a) bulk g-C3N4; (b) g-C3N4 nanosheets; (c) bulk g-C3N4/NS-CQDs and (d) g-C3N4 nanosheets/NS-CQDs.
(a) (b) TEM image of 7NSC-g; (c) elemental mapping images of 7NSC-g, (d) N-element and (e) S-element.
The oxidation reaction of TMB catalyzed by g-C3N4/NS-CQDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of different reaction systems; (b) UV–Vis absorption spectra of different peroxidase-like materials; (c) UV–Vis absorption spectra of g-C3N4 with different NS-CQDs doping and (d) its time kinetic curve (λ = 652 nm).
Effect of pH (a), temperature (b), reaction time (c) and substrate concentration (d) on the catalytic performance of 7NSC-g nanoenzyme.

The steady-state kinetic curves of 7NSC-g nanase, the kinetic curves of H2O2 (a) and the double reciprocal curve (b) at a fixed TMB concentration of 0.1 mM; Kinetic curve (c) and double reciprocal curve (d) of TMB at fixed H2O2 concentration of 5 mM.
Cyclic voltammetry curves of 7NSC-g in different electrolytes.
(a) Conversion of terephthalic acid (TA) to 2-hydroxy-terephthalic acid (2OH-TA) with high fluorescence; (b) Fluorescence spectra of different concentrations of 7NSC-g added to TA+H2O2 system.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum with H2O2 concentration; (b) Detecting the concentration change curve of H2O2 and the corresponding standard curve.
Mechanism diagram of glucose detection by 7NSC-g.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum with glucose concentration; (b) To detect the curve of glucose concentration change and the corresponding standard curve.
(a) The selectivity of 7NSC-g to glucose; (b) 7NSC-g was used to detect the stability of glucose.
K m and V max comparison of nanozymes.
The linear range and LOD of H2O2 were measured by nano-enzymes of different materials.
The content of H2O2 in milk was determined.
The linear range and LOD of glucose were measured by nanomases of different materials.
The amount of glucose in urine was measured.
Comparison with other literature LOD.

row.entry: the resultant detection limit of glucose was 1.63 μΜ
[1] Sun J, Li C, Qi Y, et al. Optimizing colorimetric assay based on V2O5 nanozymes for sensitive detection of H2O2 and glucose. Sensors, 2016, 16(4): 584.

[2] Mei, He, et al. “Fluorescent and visual assay of H2O2 and glucose based on a highly sensitive copper nanoclusters-Ce (III) fluoroprobe.” Analytical and Bioanalytical Chemistry 413 (2021): 2135–2146.

[3] Cao J, Jiang H, Wu Y, et al. Visual detection of H2O2 and glucose by HBcAb-HRP fluorescence-enhanced CdTe QDs/CDs ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform. Colloids and Surfaces B: Biointerfaces, 2024, 235: 113774.
[4] Ge C, Lu W, Sun X, et al. Microwave-assisted one-pot synthesis of Ag NPs/C and its application in H2O2 and glucose detection. Chemical Research in Chinese Universities, 2016, 32(3): 433–436.

[5] Chen H, Fang A, He L, et al. Sensitive fluorescent detection of H2O2 and glucose in human serum based on inner filter effect of squaric acid-iron (III) on the fluorescence of upconversion nanoparticle. Talanta, 2017, 164: 580–587.

[6] Chen P, Zheng C, Chen C, et al. Thiol inhibition of Hg cold vapor generation in SnCl2/NaBH4 system: a homogeneous bioassay for H2O2/glucose and butyrylcholinesterase/pesticide sensing by atomic spectrometry. Analytica Chimica Acta, 2020, 1111: 8–15.

[7] Yu H, Wu H, Tian X, et al. A nano-sized Cu-MOF with high peroxidase-like activity and its potential application in colorimetric detection of H2O2 and glucose. RSC advances, 2021, 11(43): 26963–26973.

[8] Mei H, Ma Y, Wu H, et al. Fluorescent and visual assay of H2O2 and glucose based on a highly sensitive copper nanoclusters-Ce (III) fluoroprobe. Analytical and Bioanalytical Chemistry, 2021, 413: 2135–2146.

[9] Tian, Long, et al. “Magnetic covalent organic framework nanospheres with enhanced peroxidase-like activity for colorimetric detection of H2O2 and glucose.”Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects666 (2023): 131309.

[10] Madhaiyan, Ramesh, et al. “A facile synthesis of CuZrO2 nanoparticle functionalized chitosan for capable and stable non-enzymatic electrochemical detection of glucose and H2O2.”New Journal of Chemistry47.32 (2023): 15099–15110.

head.ce:title: Preparation of g-C3N4/NS-CQDs composites and their application in the detection of hydrogen peroxide and glucose

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Heilongjiang University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Harbin, Heilongjiang Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Heilongjiang University of Traditional Chinese Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Mudanjiang Cancer Hospital, Mudanjiang, Heilongjiang Province, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Mudanjiang Cancer Hospital, Mudanjiang, Heilongjiang Province, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong College of Chinese Medicine, Yantai, Shandong Province, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Green Building Materials, China State Building Materials, Research Institute Co, Ltd. Room, Chaoyang District, Beijing, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Green Building Materials
China State Building Materials, Research Institute Co, Ltd. Room

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Green Building Materials, China State Building Materials, Research Institute Co, Ltd. Room, Chaoyang District, Beijing, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Based on the excellent properties of g-C3N4, co-doped carbon quantum dots (NS-CQDs) were selected to modify it in order to enhance its peroxidase-like activity, and the synthesized NS-CQDs/g-C3N4 nano-enzymes were applied to the detection of common small molecules, such as hydrogen peroxide and glucose, to provide theoretical basis for the construction of g-C3N4 nano-enzymes-based biosensors. FT-IR, SEM, TEM, and other characterization methods determined the composition and morphology of NS-CQDs/g-C3N4. This paper discusses the influence of different morphologies on composites’ catalytic activity. According to the fluorescence spectrum, thecatalytic mechanismfollowsthe hydroxyl radicalmechanism. Under the optimal experimental conditions, the prepared composites were used to detect glucose, and the resultant detection limit of glucose was 1.63 μΜ. The results showed that NS-CQDs/g-C3N4 outperforms most of the nanoenzymes of similar materials, which provides a new idea to broaden its application in nanoenzymes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoenzymes are more accessible to produce than natural enzymes, more cost-effective, and have higher catalytic stability in various biomedical applications [1,2]. By adjusting the morphology and composition, Nano-enzymes can improve their catalytic activity to the same level as natural enzymes. Carbon-based nanomaterials are rich in surface functional groups and easy to prepare. C is the second largest constituent element in the human body and plays a vital role in human physiological synthesis, so carbon-based nanoenzymes have a broader application prospect [3–6]. Carbon-based nanomaterials are rich in surface functional groups, easy to prepare, and essential to human physiological synthesis. Among the family of carbon-based nanomaterials, g-C3N4 has become a hot research topic due to its high chemical stability, adjustable electronic structure, and abundant and inexpensive raw materials for preparation [7,8]. It has been demonstrated that g-C3N4 has relatively low peroxidase-like catalytic activity, and therefore, it needs to be further modified to prepare more effective peroxidase mimics [9]. We have found that small-sized carbon-based nanomaterials often exhibit unique optical properties. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are carbon materials smaller than 10 nm in all three dimensions [10–12]. Compared to conventional semiconductor quantum dots formed based on heavy metal, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have the advantages of chemical inertness, resistance to photobleaching, low toxicity, and biocompatibility [13–15].

The fluorescence quenching phenomenon occurs in the presence of electron donor and acceptor molecules, demonstrating the potential of carbon quantum dots as oxidizing and reducing agents by acting as electron donors and acceptors. In addition, the combination of the attempted effect, small size effect, and surface effect gives carbon quantum dots unique properties in terms of physical properties such as light, electricity, sound, heat, and magnetism, as well as chemical properties such as catalysis and molecular recognition [5,16–18]. The results show that doping electron-rich N atoms in carbon matrix materials can change the electron arrangement and increase the electron-giving ability to produce active sites with high catalytic activity. The doping of S elements will change the surface groups and conjugation system of CQDs, thereby improving the optical properties of CQDs [19–21]. Based on the above basis, the preparation of N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots (NS-CQDs) was chosen for the composite modification of g-C3N4, Fig. 1 .

In this study, NS-CQDs/g-C3N4 binary nanocomposites were designed and successfully prepared for the first time. Due to the π-π interaction between NS-CQDs and g-C3N4, as well as the fact that small-sized NS-CQDs can form a uniformly distributed and tight interface with g-C3N4 after composite, they can effectively separate the photo-generated electron-hole pairs and thus improve their peroxidase-like catalytic activity. The colorimetric sensing performance of NS-CQDs/g-C3N4 was evaluated and explored using colorimetric detection of H2O2 and glucose [22–25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of different forms of g-C3N4
ce:section.ce:para: Preparation of bulk g-C3N4: According to the relevant literature reports, the thermal polymerization method was used to prepare bulk g-C3N4. Firstly, 10 g of urea was in a corundum crucible, and the crucible was put into a muffle furnace and calcined at 5 ℃/min for 3 h at a rate of 5 ℃, Then, the samples were taken out and milled after cooling to obtain the yellow bulk g-C3N4 powder.
Preparation of g-C3N4 nanosheets:
Take 2 g of yellow lumpy g-C3N4 powder in a corundum crucible.
Please place it in a muffle furnace at 500 ℃ calcined for 3 h, heating rate 5 ℃/min, after cooling, after sufficiently milling to get yellowish powder.

Add the obtained product to the appropriate amount of anhydrous ethanol solution in ultrasonication for 2 h to make it sufficiently peeled off, then centrifuged the mixture of solutions after 75 ℃ drying to get g-C3N4 nanosheets.
The optimal morphology was determined by investigating the catalytic activity of g-C3N4 with different morphologies.

Accurately weigh 0.5 g of L-cysteine and dissolve in 30 mL of deionized water, stirring until dissolved. 1 mL of 0.1 mol/L NaOH solution was added and stirred for another 20 min. The solution was transferred to a high-pressure reactor and reacted at 120 °C for 6 h. A dark yellow carbon quantum dot solution was obtained, first filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane, and the filtrate was taken and then dialyzed in a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 1000 Da. Dialysis was carried out under magnetic stirring at 25 °C with a stirring speed of 60 r/min, and the water was changed every 12 h for a total of 4 days of dialysis. The inner liquid of the dialysis bag was collected to obtain the purified NS-CQDs solution.

Fig. 2 shows that g-C3N4 with different morphologies has two prominent diffraction peaks at 13.08 and 27.38. The strong peak at 27.38 corresponds to the structural stack between g-C3N4 graphite layers. The weak diffraction peak around 13.08 corresponds to the distance of the “nitrogen hole” formed by polymerizing the repeated structural unit triazine in the layer. The XRD pattern of NS-CQDs shows a broad diffraction peak around 2θ = 21 [26,27]. It shows that the prepared samples are amorphous carbon materials with poor crystallinity, which accords with the XRD pattern of carbon quantum dots in conventional research. This proves that the composition and crystallinity of the prepared sample nanoparticles have met the carbon dots' characteristics. We can see that the NS-CQDs/g-C3N4 composite keeps the crystal structure of g-C3N4 well. The strong peak of the g-C3N4 monomer at 2θ = 27.8 is attributed to the (002) diffraction peak, which indicates the interlayer stacking structure of the conjugated aromatic system. The weak diffraction peak at 13.6 comes from the in-plane structure of tri-triazine, and a weak diffraction peak belonging to NS-CQDs also appears at 21.6. With the increase of NS-CQDs' content, the position of the (002) diffraction peak shifted slightly to a lower angle. (002) The diffraction peak's sharpness increases with the lattice distance of the layered structure. This phenomenon indicates the successful coupling of NS-CQD and g-C3N4. In addition, the diffraction peak of NS-CQDs is not evident in the XRD pattern of NS-CQDs /g-C3N4 catalyst. This is mainly due to the low crystallinity of NS-CQDs and its high dispersion on the surface of g-C3N4. With the increase of NS-CQDs content, the position of the (002) diffraction peak is slightly blueshifted toward a lower angular value, corresponding to the increase of the lattice distance of the lamellar structure, and the sharpness of the (002) diffraction peak is obviously strengthened, and the lattice index is the strongest for the (002) diffraction peak sharpness when the doping amount of NS-CQDs is 7 %, i.e., the atoms in the crystals are more orderly, which indicates that the g-C3N4 nanosheets have a larger specific surface area can be better doped, which is conducive to the uniform distribution of NS-CQDs on their surfaces, improving the stability and reducing the agglomeration phenomenon of quantum dots.

Fig. 3 shows the FT-IR spectra of a series of materials. Analysis of the infrared spectrum of NS-CQDs in Fig. 3 shows that at 3453 cm−1, there is an absorption peak caused by the stretching vibration of hydroxyl (–OH) on the surface of NS-CQDs. Rich –OH makes NS-CQDs have good water solubility. We found the stretching vibration absorption peaks of typical carbon-hydrogen bonds (C-H) at 2958 and 2928 cm−1. The absorption peaks at 1542 and 1056 cm−1 represent the vibration of carbon–oxygen double bond (C=O) and carbon–oxygen single bond (C-O). The absorption peak between 1349 and 1435 cm−1 is produced by the stretching vibration of the amino group (–NH2), carbon–nitrogen bond (C-N), and a carboxyl group (–COOH).In addition, the absorption peaks at 2426 and 1111 cm−1 are attributed to the vibration of the sulfhydryl group (S-H) and carbon–sulfur bond (C-S), consistent with the literature report. The results showed that NS-CQDs were successfully synthesized. We can find that there is little difference between FTIR spectra of g-C3N4 and NS-CQDs/g-C3N4 composites from Fig. 3 a. Several absorption peaks in the range of 1120–1710 cm-1 come from the C-N tensile vibration in the aromatic ring, which proves that there are N-(C)3 and C-NH-C units in the composite, forming C-N-C bonds. The absorption peak at 810 cm−1 is the bending vibration peak of the triazine ring, the basic structural unit of g-C3N4. This shows that the graphite phase nitrided carbon-based materials have been successfully synthesized, and the chemical structures of g-C3N4 and NS-CQDs/g-C3N4 are similar. Fig. 3 b shows that a series of NSC-g materials all have –NH stretching vibration broadband belonging to g-C3N4 in the 3000–3500 cm−1 range, and all have similar absorption peaks. The vibration bending degree of the carbon skeleton increases with the increase of NS-CQDs loading. However, the characteristic vibration of g-C3N4 gradually weakened, which indicated that NS-CQDs/g-C3N4 composites were successfully prepared. In addition, the characteristic absorption at 1625 cm−1 is associated with a C=C stretching mode, which can be attributed to the photoluminescence of NS-CQDs induced by π-π* leaps, whereas g-C3N4 with an aryl-ring structure is π-rich, which can provide more activation sites through π-π stacking interactions.

ObtainRaman information to analyze further the information of different carbon states insidethecarbonpoint. Fig. 4 shows that two distinct peaks are observed for NS-CQDs. The peak between 700 and 800 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibrational deformation of the benzene ring structure or the isomer of S4. 1500–1600 cm−1, the peaks of G-band (sp2 hybridization) and D-band (sp3 hybridization) fuse, probably due to the disordered nature of the reaction. sp2 belongs to the heterocyclic aromatic carbons in the heterocyclic form, which can be attributed to benzene ring or aromatic ring structure containing N heterocycle in the NS-CQDs. In contrast, sp2belongs to the heterocyclic form. Alternatively aromatic ring structures such as N-containing heterocycles, and sp3hybridization imply the presence of multiple functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots. In addition, the NH2 shear vibrational modes, C-H planar bending and C-N stretching vibrational modes disappear in o-PDs, which suggests that intermolecular polymerization and cleavage reaction processes exist during the reaction.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measures the chemical composition of materials, the valence states of elements, and the qualitative and quantitative analysis of elements. The XPS spectrum of 7NSC-g shows the existence of N, C, O, and S, further proving the existence of NS-CQDs and g-C3N4 in the composite (Fig. 5 ).

The high-resolution spectrogram five a of C 1 s has three peaks at 284.84 ev, 286.4 ev, and 288.24 ev, corresponding to C=C, N-C=N, C-O; the previous research speculated that the hydroxyl group (–OH) on g-C3N4 and NS-CQDs might be re-bonded by losing an H2O molecule to form a C-O-C bond. The high-resolution spectrogram of N 1 s has three prominent peaks at 398.7 ev, 399.84 ev, and 401.1 EV, which correspond to the sp2 bond N-C=N, N-C3, and C-NH (amino) in the triazine ring, respectively. The peaks of the S 2p spectrum at the binding energies of 162.5 eV and 163.84 eV correspond to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively. There are characteristic peaks at 531.6 eV and 533.3 eV in the spectrum of O1s. These two characteristic peaks belong to the C=O bond and C-O bond, respectively; we can prove that NS-CQDs were successfully loaded on the surface of g-C3N4.
The morphologies of massive g-C3N4, g-C3N4 nanosheets, massive g-C3N4/NS-CQDs, and g-C3N4 nanosheets /NS-CQDs were observed by scanning electron microscope.

Fig. 7 shows that the massive g-C3N4 prepared by calcining urea precursor presents aggregated massive materials, as reported. However, in Fig. 6
b, many-layered g-C3N4 can be observed. The curling phenomenon appears because of the thin lamellae and its morphology is similar to that of flaky graphene or graphene oxide. It shows that g-C3N4 nanosheets were successfully peeled off. At the same time, the effect of composite NS-CQDs on the morphology of materials was compared. Comparing Fig. 7 c and d, we can see that the g-C3N4 nanosheet /NS-CQDs doped with NS-CQDS still maintain a thin lamellar structure, have a large specific surface area and can adsorb more substrates. However, the bulk g-C3N4 doped with NS-CQDs shows an apparent bulk structure, although the pores are increased.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transmission electron microscopy and energy spectrum analysis
ce:section.ce:para: According to the TEM image, the prepared 7NSC-g sample with a thin sheet structure and transparent characteristics (Fig. 8 a) shows that the composite material with ultra-thin lamellar g-C3N4 as the main body has been prepared after secondary calcination and ultrasonic treatment. Because the relative contrast between NS-CQDs and g-C3N4 lamellae is weak, it is impossible to observe the existence of NS-CQDs in Fig. 7 b. The mapping surface scan (7c, D, E) of 7NSC-g shows that the composite nano-materials of 7NSC-g contain S and distribute uniformly with N, which proves that NS-CQDs is successfully loaded on the surface of g-C3N4 nano-sheet.

Moreover, g-C3N4 is rich in π, which can adsorb more TMB through π-π stacking interaction. The present research shows that TMB reacts with H2O2 in the first step catalyzed by peroxidase. The amino group loses an electron and becomes a cationic free radical in the system as a dimeric charge transfer complex. This intermediate shows a stronger affinity for negatively charged carbon dots; therefore, loading NS-CQDs on g-C3N4 nanosheets can adsorb more TMB, thus increasing the peroxidase-like activity of the composites.

At the same time, we compared the peroxidase-like activity of nano-quantum dot complexes with different doping levels and studied their time kinetics. The result shows that 7NSC-g has the best peroxidase-like activity (Fig. 10 c, D). In this process, when the doping amount of NS-CQDs is 7 %(7NSC-g), it shows the best catalytic activity. Therefore, we choose 7NSC-g as peroxidase for the follow-up experiment.

Similar to the natural enzyme horseradish peroxidase (HRP), the catalytic ability of 7NSC-g will be affected by external conditions to some extent. Therefore, it is necessary to optimize the conditions such as pH and temperature to achieve the maximum catalytic capacity of 7NSC-G. The wavelength was fixed at 652 nm, and the maximum absorbance of enzyme activity was set at 100 %. Compared with other absorbance values, the relative activity was measured in parallel 3 times in each experiment group, using the average as a chart. Fig. 11 shows that when the pH is 2–9, the enzyme activity first increases and then decreases. When pH=4, the enzyme's activity reached the maximum, indicating that the activity was better under acidic conditions, consistent with HRP and most reported nano-enzymes.

Similarly, to investigate the effect of temperature on the enzyme's catalytic activity, the enzyme's activity increased with the temperature increase in the range of 15-60℃ (Fig. 11 b). When the temperature reaches 35℃, it reaches the maximum value, and when the temperature reaches 40℃, 7NSC-g still shows excellent catalytic activity. The catalytic activity of 7NSC-g is insensitive to temperature compared with HRP, which gives it a specific application value. Fig. 11 c shows the effect of reaction time on the enzyme's catalytic activity. With the extension of reaction time, the catalytic activity of 7NSC-g gradually increased, and the catalytic activity reached the maximum when the reaction time reached 35 min. However, after 25 min of reaction, the growth rate tended to be flat with little change, so it was enough to react for 25 min in the experimental process. In the meantime, the substrate concentration will also affect the enzyme activity, changing the substrate TMB concentration within the range of 2–12 mM; when the concentration of TMB exceeds eight mM, the catalytic activity of 7NSC-g gradually decreases (Fig. 10 d). Therefore, the optimum substrate concentration was determined to be eight mM. To summarize, pH=4, temperature 35℃, reaction time 25 min, and TMB concentration eight mM were selected as the best experimental conditions.

In order to further explore the catalytic mechanism of g-C3N4 nanosheets/NS-CQDs nanoenzyme, the catalytic kinetic process was explored. As shown in Fig. 12 . Based on the double-substrate reaction mechanism of POD, we fixed the concentration of one substrate, TMB or H2O2, while changing the concentration of the other substrate under the optimal reaction conditions. We obtained the steady-state kinetic parameters of the nano-enzyme (7NSC-g) POD activity. Km and Vmax are obtained by fitting the Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal curve and substituting it into the Michaelis equation. The smaller the Km value, the stronger the enzyme's affinity to the substrate. Table 1 shows the results of kinetic parameters. By comparing the Km of 7NSC-g to TMB and H2O2,7NSC-g nanoenzyme has a stronger affinity for TMB substrate; because the catalytic activity of nanoenzyme is closely related to the material structure, on the one hand, g-C3N4 nanosheets, the primary material of 7NSC-g, have rich void structure and large surface area. It is beneficial for absorbing more substrates and providing more active sites. On the other hand, it has been reported that NS-CQDs have a negative charge on their surface, while TMB exists in a molecular state with a positive charge in an acidic medium. Therefore, NS-CQDs are loaded on the surface of g-C3N4 nanosheets, and more TMB is adsorbed by electrostatic action, so the affinity for TMB is stronger.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the basic reaction mechanism of g-C3N4/NS-CQDs composites
Cyclic voltammetry was used to determine electron transfer
ce:section.ce:para: Some scholars have proposed that the catalytic reaction mechanism of partially simulated peroxidase comes from charge transfer. In order to study the catalytic mechanism of g-C3N4/NS-CQDs composites. We insert 7NSC-g modified ITO electrode into 0.2 M NaAc-HAc buffer solution with pH=4.0 and 0.2 M NaAc-HAc buffer solution with pH=4.0 added with 1 M H2O2. Fig. 13 shows the electrocatalytic results, and no current change was observed in the two electrolyte solutions, indicating that the catalytic mechanism of the g-C3N4/NS-CQDs composite does not follow the electron transfer principle.

In addition to the electron transfer mechanism, some peroxidase reaction mechanisms are considered the reaction principle in which H2O2 is decomposed to produce OH. This mechanism is based on the principle of a Fenton-like reaction. Scholars widely agree that OH produced by decomposition can oxidize TMB to produce blue product ox TMB. Terephthalic acid (TA) is a non-fluorescent substance that reacts with OH to generate a product with solid fluorescence, 2- hydroxy terephthalic acid (2OH-TA) (Fig. 14 a). The more OH produced during the catalytic reaction, the stronger the fluorescence intensity. Adding 7NSC-g with different concentrations into the TA+H2O2 reaction system during the experiment, with the increase of the concentration of 7NSC-g, the fluorescence intensity increases (Fig. 14 b), so the catalytic mechanism of g-C3N4/NS-CQDs as a POD-like material follows the hydroxyl radical mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of hydrogen peroxide and actual samples

ce:section.ce:para: The spectra of TMB+H2O2 + 7NSC-g reaction systems with different concentration gradients of H2O2 were scanned by UV spectrophotometer under optimal experimental conditions. Fig. 15 a shows the ultraviolet absorption spectrum changing with H2O2 concentration; Fig. 15 b shows that the absorbance at λ = 652 nm increases with the increase of H2O2 concentration in the solution. There is a linear relationship between absorbance and H2O2 concentration in the range of 10–100 μM A=0.0024C H2O2 + 0.0907(R2 = 0.9921), and the detection limit is 0.043μ m. They compared Table 2 , which shows that 7NSC-g has a more comprehensive linear range and lower detection limit than other similar peroxidase mimetic enzymes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of hydrogen peroxide in milk
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the excellent performance of 7NSC-g in detecting H2O2, it was applied to detect H2O2 in milk samples. Calculate its accuracy and precision by adding a standard method. Adding different concentrations of H2O2(25, 50, and 75 μM) into diluted milk samples to obtain spiked milk, The recovery rate is 99.84 %∼100.37 %, and the relative standard deviation is 1.08 ∼ 2.47 %, which shows that the method is reliable in practical detection. (Table 3 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glucose and actual samples

ce:section.ce:para: Glucose oxidase (GOx) can catalyze glucose to generate gluconic acid and H2O2. Therefore, the content of glucose can be indirectly detected by measuring the absorption value at λ = 652 nm. The determination principle is shown in Fig. 16 , and the colorimetric detection of glucose can be realized according to the corresponding absorbance change of the reaction system.

In order to further evaluate the selectivity of 7NSC-g in detecting glucose, the effects of glucose analogs such as Fructose, Lactose, and Maltose on the determination results were studied. Results As shown in Fig. 18 , under the same experimental conditions, only glucose caused significant absorbance. The absorbance of glucose is much higher than that of the control group, even if the concentration of interfering substances is five times higher than that of glucose. This may be because glucose can be decomposed into H2O2 under glucose oxidase. TMB is oxidized under the catalysis of 7NSC-g, while other sugars are not oxidized and decomposed into H2O2 by glucose oxidase. The above glucose analogs have little effect on glucose detection, indicating that the prepared 7NSC-g has good selectivity for glucose. At the same time, we investigated the stability of the material. As shown in Fig. 17 b, the activity of the material can remain above 86.4 % after four weeks of continuous testing, which shows that the material has good stability.

In order to investigate the feasibility of the proposed 7NSC-g in the actual sample detection, the glucose concentration in the diluted simulated artificial urine sample was further detected after adding glucose. Table 5 shows that the recovery rate of this method is less than 104.5 % and RSD is less than 3.75, which indicates that the colorimetric detection method based on this nano-enzyme is reliable in the actual urine sample.
We have compared this paper with the previously studied articles and found that this paper has better detection results. Table 6

This chapter prepared g-C3N4/NS-CQDs composites using the hydrothermal method and explored the peroxidase-like activity of the composites. The massive g-C3N4 was stripped into nano-flake g-C3N4 through secondary calcination and ultrasonic treatment. This helped expand the specific surface area and expose more surface active sites, showing better POD-like activity. On this basis, the g-C3N4/NS-CQDs composite was synthesized by introducing NS-CQDs, and this paper discusses the influence of different doping amounts of NS-CQDs on the enzyme activity of the composite. The experimental results show that the composite shows the maximum catalytic activity when the doped NS-CQDs is 7 %. Its steady-state kinetics conform to the Michaelis-Menten model, showing a stronger affinity for TMB due to the electrostatic effect of NS-CQDs. Under the optimum experimental conditions of pH=4, temperature 35℃, reaction time 25 min, and TMB concentration 0.03 mM, and use 7NSC-g to detect hydrogen peroxide. There is an excellent linear relationship in the range of 10–100 μM, and the detection limit is 0.043 μM, and we applied it to the detection of hydrogen peroxide in milk samples. When it is used to detect glucose, it shows an excellent linear relationship within 10–400 μM; the detection limit is 1.63 μM, lower than most nano-enzymes, and it has good selectivity and practicability.

This work was financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province grant number [LH2019H055], and the APC was funded by [Haiyang Liu]. 2019 Hei-longjiang University of Traditional Chinese Medicine “Outstanding Young Backbone Teachers Support Program” Program.

Haiyang Liu: Resources, Funding acquisition. Zhe Wang: Validation. Lijun Jin: Software, Investigation. Lixia Jin: Resources. Fangjie Yan: Data curation. Ji Li: . Kuilin Lv: Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant no. 52102119, Young Elite Scientists Sponsorship Program by CAST (No. 2022QNRC001).
